sections.markdown:
# DEFSYSTEM Basics

The canonical `.asd` file:

```lisp
(asdf:defsystem #:cool
  :description "A small example system."
  :author      "You <you@example.com>"
  :license     "MIT"
  :version     "0.1.0"
  :depends-on  (#:alexandria)
  :serial      t
  :components  ((:file "package")
                (:file "core")))
```

The required keys are `:components` (what files), and almost-required `:depends-on` (other systems). Everything else is metadata.

* `:depends-on` — a list of system names. ASDF loads each before this system. They go through the same registry, so `#:alexandria` will be found by Quicklisp or wherever else it lives.
* `:components` — a list of component descriptors. The most common is `(:file "NAME")` referring to `NAME.lisp` (no extension; ASDF supplies it). Others include `(:module ...)` (a subdirectory) and `(:static-file ...)` (data files copied alongside).
* `:serial t` — load components in the order written. Each implicitly depends on the previous one. Without `:serial`, you must declare dependencies per component with `:depends-on`.

When ASDF compiles a file (the `compile-op`), the artefact lands in a per-implementation cache (`~/.cache/common-lisp/...`), keyed by source path and SBCL version. Edit a file and reload, and only it (plus things that depend on it) is recompiled.

sections.explain: A trivial system with no components. ASDF still registers it; FIND-SYSTEM returns its object.
An empty :DEPENDS-ON. SYSTEM-DEPENDS-ON reads it back.

sections.prompt: Define a system named MINI-SYS with no components, no dependencies, description "mini". Then define ASD-DESC (NAME) returning the description string of system NAME (a keyword or string).
